Chapter 22
Endocrine and multisystemic disease
Lauren Powers
Selected endocrine diseases Thyroid gland
The thyroid gland is situated along the ventral cervical 
Endocrine diseases are considered relatively uncommon 
area ventral to the trachea. It is bilobed and the lobes are 
in guinea pigs when compared with other mammalian 
connected by an isthmus. The thyroid gland is not palpable 
species. However, there are several endocrine diseases 
in the normal guinea pig. Histologically, the two main cell 
of significant clinical importance in guinea pigs, most 
types are the follicular cells, which are the predominant

crine glands. supported by connective tissue. Unencapsulated nests of 
thyroid follicular cells may also be seen in the ventral 
cervical region adjacent to the thyroid glands in healthy 
guinea pigs.
Hyperthyroidism and thyroid neoplasia
Thyroid neoplasia and hyperthyroidism have been reported 
Pituitary gland in guinea pigs, with a prevalence rate for thyroid neoplasia 
on post-mortem examination of 2.1% in one recent retro-
Thyroid gland spective study (Bertram et al., 2025) and 3.6% in another 
study (Gibbons et al., 2013). In one report (Nešpor et al., 
2023), thyroid adenocarcinoma was the most common 
tumour type in a total of 153 neoplasms from biopsy and 
post-mortem samples from guinea pigs. Reported neo-
plasms include papillary adenoma, cystic thyroid adenoma, 
thyroid fibroblastic sarcoma, ectopic carcinoma, thyroid 
cystadenoma, ossifying thyroid carcinoma and thyroid 
Pancreas
adenocarcinoma, among others. In one retrospective 
Adrenal gland study, 7 of 19 (36.8%) of thyroid neoplasms in guinea pigs 
were adenocarcinomas (Gibbons et al., 2013); the median 
age of guinea pigs with confirmed thyroid neoplasia was 
4.3 years in this report (Figure 22.2). In a separate report 
(Nešpor et al., 2023), the mean age of affected guinea pigs 
was 4.4 years with an age range of 2 to 8 years and males 
more often affected at 78.57%. Distant metastasis is rare 
but has been documented in the draining cervical lymph 
nodes, lungs, heart base and elsewhere in guinea pigs 
(Bertram et al., 2025).
Hyperthyroidism is defined as increased production 
and secretion of thyroid hormones with associated clinical

Chapter 22 • Endocrine and multisystemic disease
trophy or neoplasia. Hyperthyroidism is very likely under-
recognized in guinea pigs. The reported median age of 
guinea pigs with clinical hyperthyroidism is 4.5 to 5 years.
Classic clinical signs of hyperthyroidism in guinea pigs 
include hyperactivity (Brandão et al., 2013; Künzel et al., 
2013) and polyphagia associated with a normal body con-
dition or weight loss (Figure 22.3). Other clinical signs 
include polyuria and polydipsia, lethargy, reduced appetite, 
progressive bilaterally symmetrical alopecia and soft stools 
or diarrhoea. Upon examination, affected guinea pigs may 
have tachycardia, heart murmur, or a visible or palpable 
ventral neck mass in the area of the thyroid gland (10 of 
19 guinea pigs in Gibbons et al., 2013) (Figure 22.4). Guinea 
pigs generally resent palpation of the ventral neck, but 
this area should always be palpated during the routine 
physical examination as masses can easily be missed. 
Absence of a palpable mass does not exclude disease, 
and the size of a mass does not necessarily indicate the 
severity of disease. Ventral cervical masses associated 
with the thyroid gland must be differentiated from odon- Weight loss and thin body condition in a 5-year-old 
22.3
togenic abscesses, cervical lymphadenitis, lymphoma and  entire boar with hyperthyroidism (top), compared with a 
other neoplasms (Figure 22.5). healthy cagemate.
The diagnosis of hyperthyroidism in guinea pigs can 
be challenging but is generally based on history, physical 
examination findings, and measurement of circulating 
thyroid hormone concentrations. However, validated thy-
roid hormone panels for guinea pigs have yet to be devel-
oped by most commercial laboratories. Measurement of 
the total thyroxine (TT4) concentration is considered by 
some authors to be potentially unreliable in guinea pigs, 
both for diagnosis and for the monitoring of therapeutic 
efficacy (Mayer et al., 2010). With early hyperthyroidism, 
guinea pigs may have normal concentrations of TT4, and 
other illness may result in low concentrations of TT4 
(euthyroid sick syndrome). Measurement of free thyroxine 
(FT4) may be more sensitive and less likely to be affected 
by concurrent illness. Reported reference intervals for TT4 
concentrations in young adult guinea pigs range from 14.2 
to 74.9 nmol/l (Figure 22.6). Hyperthyroidism in guinea pigs 
was defined as a TT4 concentration of greater than 66.9 
nmol/l in one report (Girod-Rüffer et al., 2019).
Reference intervals for thyroid stimulating hormone 
(TSH) in healthy pet guinea pigs have recently been pub- Visible thyroid mass along the ventral neck in a 5-year-
22.4
lished (Jähnig et al., 2023). An experimental study evalu- old entire boar with hyperthyroidism.
ated the use of recombinant human TSH (rhTSH) for 
evaluation of thyroid function in 10 healthy adult guinea 
pigs. 100 μg of rhTSH was administered intramuscularly, 
and plasma TT4 concentrations were evaluated before 
and at 3 and 4 hours after administration. Results from this 
study indicated that the TT4 concentration should at least 
double after 3 to 4 hours in healthy, euthyroid guinea pigs 
(Mayer et al., 2013). A separate report (Jähnig et al., 2023) 
evaluated the thyrotropin-releasing hormone (TRH)-
stimulation test in four healthy pet guinea pigs using 0.2 
mg/ml TRH Ferring® (protirelin, a synthetic TRH) at 10 µg/
kg i.m. with repeat blood collection at 30 minutes and 120 
minutes post injection. The TT4 increased 1.4- to 1.79-fold, 
the FT4 increased 1.0- to 1.34-fold, and the TSH increased 
1.5- to 2.14-fold after administration in these guinea pigs.
Additional diagnostics for evaluating the thyroid gland 
in guinea pigs include imaging such as conventional radi-
ography and ultrasonography (Figure 22.7) as well as cross-
sectional imaging such as computed tomography (CT) and 
magnetic resonance imaging (MRI). Osseous metaplasia of 
thyroid neoplasms was observed in 8 of 19 cases in one 
report (Gibbons et al., 2013) and in 5 out of 28 cases in a 
An adult entire sow with thyroid neoplasia involving the 
separate report (Nešpor et al., 2023). Ossified neoplasms  22.5 left thyroid lobe (arrowed) and an enlarged right 
may be visible radiographically (Figure 22.8). Thyroid lobe  submandibular lymph node affected by lymphoma (arrowhead).

dimensions measured by ultrasonography and CT were 
recently established in 20 adult pet guinea pigs (Pelligra et 
al., 2025). Echogenicity and homogeneity were assessed 
by ultrasonography and attenuation values and homogene-
ity were assessed by CT. The thyroid gland is frequently 
hypoechoic on ultrasonography. The use of scintigraphy 
for the diagnosis of thyroid disease in guinea pigs has also 
been described. Fine-needle aspiration (FNA) with cytology 
may be helpful in the evaluation of thyroid masses. However, 
surgical incisional or excisional biopsy and histopathology 
may be needed for a definitive diagnosis.
Treatment of hyperthyroidism can be equally challeng-
ing in guinea pigs. Treatment options include oral or 
transdermal thyreostatic drugs, surgical thyroidectomy 
Right lateral radiograph showing an ossifying thyroid 
and radioactive iodine. In a published case report series  22.8 carcinoma in the 5-year-old entire boar with hyper-
(Girod-Rüffer et al., 2019), 13/27 (48%) guinea pigs treated  thyroidism depicted in Figure 22.3. The mass is visible radio-
only with oral thyreostatic drugs showed clinical improve- graphically along the ventral neck.
ment. There are few reports regarding survival time with 
zole have yet been reported. However, there are no phar-
medical therapy alone, but in one report (Künzel et al., 
macological (pharmacokinetic or pharmacodynamic) 
2013), two animals survived for 18 and 28 months. No 
studies on this use of these drugs in guinea pigs. Dosages 
adverse effects to thiamazole (methimazole) or carbima-
for individual guinea pigs should be based on clinical 
response and periodic thyroid hormone measurement to 
monitor response to treatment (see also Appendix 2). The 
successful use of radioactive iodine therapy (131I) has been 
described in guinea pigs. Radiation therapy for thyroid 
neoplasia in guinea pigs has not been reported.
Surgical excision is feasible, but caution is warranted 
with surgery (Figure 22.9). Iatrogenic damage to important 
regional anatomical structures such as the parathyroid 
glands, recurrent laryngeal nerves, jugular veins and carotid 
arteries is possible, and incomplete excision has been 
described. Guinea pigs may possess ectopic thyroid tissue, 
and some normal animals are able to generate thyroid tis-
sue after thyroidectomy (Kromka and Hoar, 1975). In one 
report (Girod-Rüffer et al., 2019), 3 of 6 guinea pigs with 
hyperthyroidism that underwent surgical thyroidectomy 
showed moderate improvement, with 1 of 6 showing com-
plete resolution of clinical signs. However, several reports 
have described postoperative death in guinea pigs that 
underwent surgical thyroidectomy, including 1 of 6 animals 
(Girod-Rüffer et al., 2019), 3 of 7 animals (Gibbons et al., 
Ultrasonographic appearance of a bilobed thyroid mass 
22.7 in the 5-year-old entire boar with hyperthyroidism  2013) and perioperative death in the single guinea pig that 
depicted in Figure 22.3. underwent surgery in a separate report (Künzel et al., 2013).

Chapter 22 • Endocrine and multisystemic disease
Hyperadrenocorticism
Hyperadrenocorticism (Cushing’s disease) is a disorder 
characterized by persistently elevated circulating cortisol 
concentrations associated with either excessive pituitary 
secretion of adrenocorticotropic hormone (ACTH), most 
often from a pituitary tumour, or excessive adrenal secre-
tion of cortisol. Hyperadrenocorticism is rarely diagnosed 
in guinea pigs and is usually associated with adrenal 
neoplasia. Adrenal masses may be unilateral or bilateral 
and are generally well circumscribed. Adrenocortical 
adenomas and adenocarcinomas have been reported in 
guinea pigs and metastasis to the lungs has been reported 
with adrenal adenocarcinoma (Bertram et al., 2025).
Clinical signs of hyperadrenocorticism reported in 
guinea pigs include bilaterally symmetrical alopecia, leth-
argy, obesity or weight loss, periocular muscle atrophy, 
abdominal distension, hepatomegaly, polydipsia and 
polyuria. Differential diagnoses for hyperadrenocorticism 
in guinea pigs include cystic ovarian disease and 
hyperthyroidism.
Surgical excision of a bilobed thyroid mass in the

To the author’s knowledge, spontaneous primary disease 
or neoplasia affecting the pituitary gland or hypothalamus 
has not been described in guinea pigs.
Selected exocrine pancreatic and 
hepatobiliary diseases
Exocrine pancreatic disease
The guinea pig pancreas is elongated, pink-red in colour, 
lobular, flat and V-shaped. It is divided into cranial and 
caudal lobes, which join at the pancreatic body. The longer 
cranial lobe extends from the pancreatic body within the 
greater omentum and ends at the curvature of the omen-
tum. The shorter caudal lobe travels along the greater 
curvature of the stomach within the omentum. There is a 
short and narrow pancreatic duct that empties into the 
duodenum about 7 cm distal to the duodenal papilla of the  (a)
common bile duct. There is no accessory pancreatic duct 
in the guinea pig. There are few reports of spontaneous 
disease of the exocrine pancreas in guinea pigs. Aging 
changes are common, including interstitial infiltration of 
adipose tissue with or without mild fibrosis. Fatty infiltra-
tion can also occur within the pancreatic islets in older 
guinea pigs. These aging changes generally have little to 
no clinical significance. To the author’s knowledge, no 
clinical assays for evaluation of pancreatic function have 
been validated in the guinea pig, although concentrations 
of amylase are reportedly higher in hairless guinea pigs 
compared with haired guinea pigs.
Hepatobiliary diseases
The liver is the largest abdominal organ in the guinea pig 
and is comprised of six separate lobes (Figure 22.10). There 
is a right, left, and middle hepatic duct that combine to  (b)
form the common hepatic duct. The gall bladder is secured  (a, b) Normal post-mortem appearance of the liver and 
22.10
to the gall bladder fossa at the quadrate lobe of the liver.  gall bladder in the guinea pig.

Chapter 22 • Endocrine and multisystemic disease
Heat stroke
The ancestors of domestic guinea pigs lived in high, cooler 
altitudes in South America. Guinea pigs are prone to devel-
oping heat stroke when exposed to temperatures of 28°C 
or greater, or even at temperatures as low as 21°C if in 
direct sunlight. Stress, exertion, late term pregnancy and 
inadequate access to fresh drinking water or reluctance 
to drink (such as when traveling to shows) increase the 
risk of heat stroke in guinea pigs. Clinical signs include 
rapid shallow breathing, weakness, hyperthermia, hyper-
salivation, collapse and death. Guinea pigs should be 
cooled as soon as possible. Cool water baths, applying 
alcohol to the foot pads and parenteral administration of 
crystalloid fluids can be used to help cool affected guinea 
pigs. The prognosis is guarded and affected guinea pigs 
must be monitored closely for response to treatment and 
complications such as rebound hypothermia.
(a) Amyloidosis
Amyloidosis has been reported in guinea pigs but is most 
often found as an incidental finding on post-mortem 
examination. Amyloidosis is a condition associated with 
deposition of amyloid A insoluble fibrils in tissues affected 
by chronic inflammatory conditions such as with pododer-
matitis and osteoarthritis, and viscera such as the kidneys, 
liver, spleen and adrenal glands, but deposits in other 
tissues can be found. The author is not aware of reports 
of pre-mortem diagnosis in the guinea pig. Despite the 
fact that the kidneys may be affected with amyloidosis, 
renal failure caused by amyloidosis has not been reported 
in guinea pigs.
Alpha-mannosidosis

in a small number of guinea pigs. Reported clinical signs

Lethal white syndrome
based on visual inspection and genealogy. Genotyping 
has not yet been validated for pet guinea pigs. Roan guinea pigs have white hairs mixed with flecks of 
Satin syndrome, or satin disease, can be seen in any  another solid colour through the white, while Dalmatian 
satin guinea pig. Up to 30% of satin guinea pigs may be  guinea pigs have white hair with dark spots. Breeding 
affected and signs often occur before 1 year of age. The  two guinea pigs that have some white in their coats risks 
syndrome is more pronounced in homozygous animals  the potential for having lethal white offspring; about 25% 
but can be observed in heterozygous individuals. Clinical  of offspring from a pairing of colour-diluted Roan or Dal-
matian guinea pigs will be homozygous for the spotted 
roan or ‘lethal factor’. Homozygosity for this allele is 
associated with the clinical condition known as ‘lethal 
white syndrome’ among guinea pig fanciers. Lethal white 
guinea pigs are born completely white with red eyes 
(Figure 22.13). Pink-eyed white guinea pigs (PEWs) are a 
separate variety, and not all PEWs are lethal whites.
Lethal white syndrome is associated with a variety of 
clinical abnormalities, including microphthalmia, anoph-
thalmia, cataracts, partial or complete blindness, partial 
or complete deafness, hypodontia, dental malocclusion, 
intestinal malabsorption and immunosuppression. The 
pathophysiology of this syndrome in guinea pigs is not 
understood but may involve defective migration of neural 
crest cells during embryogenesis. Although the lifespan 
is shortened, some affected guinea pigs can survive for 
2 to 3 years, perhaps longer, with excellent husbandry 
and care. Guinea pigs affected by dental malocclusion 
may require periodic odontoplasty and regular assisted
